clinicalStem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a sudden-onset severe headache that she describes as 'the worst headache of my life', which reached maximal intensity within seconds during moderate physical exertion. She is alert and orientated, with a GCS of 15. Her BP is 178/102 mmHg. Neurological examination reveals nuchal rigidity but no focal deficits. Non-contrast CT head performed within 6 hours of onset is reported as normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
explanation: A normal CT within 6 hours does not exclude subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH); sensitivity of CT falls over time, and up to 2–5% of SAH cases are CT-negative early. Lumbar puncture looking for xanthochromia (yellow discolouration from haemoglobin breakdown products) is the gold-standard next step when CT is negative in a patient with a classic thunderclap headache presentation. Xanthochromia develops 2–4 hours after bleeding and persists for up to 2 weeks.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI with gadolinium is useful for detecting aneurysms and subacute bleeds but is not the immediate next step; LP is required to exclude SAH when CT is negative.
Lumbar puncture for xanthochromia is correct — it is the gold-standard investigation when CT is negative in a suspected SAH case.
CT cerebral angiography is performed AFTER SAH has been confirmed to locate the source; it is not the next step before diagnosis is established.
Repeating CT in 24 hours would cause dangerous diagnostic delay in a potentially life-threatening condition.
Transcranial Doppler assesses vasospasm, a complication of confirmed SAH; it has no role in primary diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: A normal CT head does NOT exclude SAH — always perform lumbar puncture for xanthochromia when clinical suspicion is high.
keyClue: Worst headache of life with nuchal rigidity and normal CT head
commonExamTrap: Assuming a normal CT rules out SAH — it does not; LP is mandatory when suspicion persists.
missedConcept: CT sensitivity for SAH decreases over time; LP is the next essential step when CT is negative.
redFlagReason: Subarachnoid haemorrhage carries up to 50% mortality; missing the diagnosis by not proceeding to LP after a normal CT is a serious and avoidable error.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old man is admitted with a confirmed aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage (Hunt and Hess grade II) due to a 7 mm left middle cerebral artery aneurysm. He is haemodynamically stable with a BP of 155/90 mmHg. CT angiography confirms the aneurysm. The neurosurgical team is planning definitive intervention. Which of the following is the most important immediate pharmacological intervention to reduce early re-bleeding risk while awaiting definitive aneurysm treatment?
options: Commence intravenous nimodipine 60 mg every 4 hours
Start prophylactic phenytoin to prevent seizures
Administer intravenous dexamethasone to reduce cerebral oedema
explanation: Nimodipine, an oral/IV calcium channel blocker, is the standard of care after aneurysmal SAH. Although it does not directly prevent re-bleeding, it significantly reduces the risk of cerebral vasospasm — the leading cause of delayed ischaemic neurological deficit — and has been shown to improve neurological outcomes. It is started immediately and continued for 21 days. Definitive aneurysm treatment (clipping or coiling) is the intervention that prevents re-bleeding.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Nimodipine is correct — it is the recommended neuroprotective agent post-SAH that reduces vasospasm and improves outcomes.
Tranexamic acid reduces re-bleeding risk modestly but increases the risk of delayed cerebral ischaemia and thrombosis; evidence does not support its routine use.
Prophylactic phenytoin is no longer routinely recommended after SAH due to evidence of worse outcomes; anticonvulsants are only given therapeutically.
Triple-H therapy (hypertension, hypervolaemia, haemodilution) is used to treat established symptomatic vasospasm, not for immediate post-SAH management.
Dexamethasone has no proven benefit in SAH and is not indicated; it is used for vasogenic oedema due to tumours.
clinicalPearl: Nimodipine 60 mg orally every 4 hours for 21 days is the cornerstone pharmacological treatment after SAH — it reduces vasospasm-related ischaemia, not re-bleeding.
keyClue: Confirmed aneurysmal SAH awaiting definitive treatment — pharmacological neuroprotection needed
commonExamTrap: Confusing nimodipine's role (anti-vasospasm) with anti-re-bleeding — nimodipine does not prevent re-bleeding but is still the priority pharmacological intervention.
missedConcept: Nimodipine reduces delayed cerebral ischaemia from vasospasm and is mandatory post-SAH; definitive aneurysm treatment prevents re-bleeding.
redFlagReason: Re-bleeding and vasospasm after SAH carry very high mortality; delay in appropriate management (nimodipine, early aneurysm securing) is life-threatening.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old woman was admitted 8 days ago with a World Federation of Neurosurgical Societies (WFNS) grade II subarachnoid haemorrhage. Her ruptured posterior communicating artery aneurysm was successfully coiled on day 2. She has been on nimodipine. Today she develops new-onset confusion, right arm weakness, and dysphasia. Her BP is 130/75 mmHg and temperature is 37.1°C. Repeat CT head shows no new haemorrhage. What is the most likely cause of her new neurological deterioration?
options: Cerebral vasospasm causing delayed cerebral ischaemia
Meningitis secondary to blood in the subarachnoid space
explanation: Delayed cerebral ischaemia (DCI) due to vasospasm is the most common serious complication after aneurysmal SAH, typically occurring between days 4–14 (peak day 7–10). The patient presents at day 8 with new focal neurological deficits after a period of stability post-coiling. Vasospasm causes arterial narrowing and downstream ischaemia, producing deficits corresponding to the affected territory. CT angiography or transcranial Doppler confirms vasospasm.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Re-bleeding from a successfully coiled aneurysm is extremely rare; new haemorrhage would also be visible on CT.
Cerebral vasospasm causing delayed cerebral ischaemia is correct — day 8 presentation with new focal deficits after a period of stability is the classic picture.
Hydrocephalus can cause confusion but typically presents earlier (acute) or as communicating hydrocephalus weeks later; new focal limb weakness and dysphasia favour ischaemia.
Hyponatraemia from cerebral salt wasting causes confusion but usually does not cause focal neurological deficits; a serum sodium should still be checked.
Meningitis is possible but would typically present with fever, meningism, and systemic features; the clinical picture here is more focal.
clinicalPearl: Any new neurological deficit between days 4–14 after SAH should be presumed vasospasm/DCI until proven otherwise.
keyClue: New focal deficits on day 8 post-SAH with no new haemorrhage on CT
commonExamTrap: Attributing day 8 deterioration to re-bleeding — re-bleeding would show on CT and is less likely after successful coiling.
missedConcept: Vasospasm peaks days 7–10 post-SAH and is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in survivors of the initial bleed.

sourceLecture: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 32-year-old man presents with 5 days of ascending weakness starting in his legs, now involving his arms and facial muscles. He had a diarrhoeal illness 3 weeks ago. On examination he has bilateral lower limb areflexia, proximal more than distal weakness (MRC grade 3/5 in both legs, 4/5 in arms), and bilateral facial weakness. His forced vital capacity (FVC) is 1.4 L (predicted 4.2 L). He states he feels short of breath when lying flat. What is the single most important immediate management step?
options: Arrange urgent intubation and mechanical ventilation assessment in HDU/ICU
Perform urgent nerve conduction studies to confirm diagnosis
explanation: In Guillain-Barré syndrome, respiratory failure is the most immediately life-threatening complication. An FVC <20 mL/kg (approximately 1.4 L in this patient against a predicted 4.2 L — this is 33% of predicted, well below the threshold), along with positional dyspnoea, signals impending respiratory failure. The '20-30-40 rule' guides intubation: FVC <20 mL/kg, maximum inspiratory pressure <30 cmH2O, or maximum expiratory pressure <40 cmH2O. Securing the airway and ICU/HDU admission takes priority over immunomodulatory therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IVIG is an appropriate disease-modifying treatment but is not the immediate priority when the patient has impending respiratory failure — airway comes first.
Urgent ICU transfer and respiratory support is correct — the FVC is critically low and positional dyspnoea indicates impending failure; ABCDE priorities apply.
Plasmapheresis is equally effective as IVIG for GBS but, like IVIG, is not the immediate priority over respiratory stabilisation.
Corticosteroids are NOT effective and may be harmful in GBS — this is a well-known trap.
Nerve conduction studies confirm the diagnosis but are not urgent when the patient has life-threatening respiratory compromise.
clinicalPearl: In GBS, monitor FVC every 4–6 hours; FVC <20 mL/kg or rapidly declining FVC mandates urgent ICU transfer and consideration of elective intubation before crisis.
keyClue: FVC 1.4 L against predicted 4.2 L (33%) with positional dyspnoea in ascending paralysis
commonExamTrap: Starting IVIG before securing the airway — disease-modifying therapy is secondary to immediate respiratory management.
missedConcept: Respiratory failure is the leading cause of death in GBS; serial FVC monitoring and early intubation threshold (20 mL/kg rule) are life-saving.
redFlagReason: GBS with FVC at 33% of predicted and positional dyspnoea indicates imminent respiratory arrest; failure to prioritise airway management is fatal.

sourceLecture: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man with a 15-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 88 mmol/mol) presents with a 12-month history of bilateral burning pain, tingling, and numbness in both feet, extending to the mid-calf. He describes the pain as worse at night, preventing sleep. Neurological examination reveals reduced vibration sense and absent ankle reflexes bilaterally. Nerve conduction studies confirm a sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy. Blood glucose optimisation is ongoing. Which pharmacological agent is most appropriate as first-line treatment for his neuropathic pain?
options: Amitriptyline 25 mg at night, titrating upward
explanation: Low-dose tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) such as amitriptyline are recommended as first-line agents for painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy by NICE and international guidelines. They modulate pain through noradrenaline and serotonin reuptake inhibition in descending pain pathways. Duloxetine (SNRI) is an equally recommended first-line option. Gabapentin and pregabalin are second-line. NSAIDs are ineffective for neuropathic pain. Opioids are generally third-line due to dependence risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Tramadol is a weak opioid with some monoamine activity; it is not first-line for diabetic neuropathy and carries dependence risk.
Amitriptyline is correct — low-dose TCA is a first-line agent for neuropathic pain in diabetic peripheral neuropathy.
Diclofenac and other NSAIDs are ineffective for neuropathic pain; they target inflammatory prostaglandins, not the central sensitisation mechanism of neuropathic pain.
Gabapentin is a valid second-line option but not preferred over TCAs as the initial choice; it is also correct per some guidelines but amitriptyline is the more classic first-line answer.
Oxycodone is a strong opioid; opioids are third-line for neuropathic pain due to limited efficacy and high dependence potential.
clinicalPearl: First-line agents for painful diabetic neuropathy are amitriptyline (TCA) or duloxetine (SNRI); gabapentinoids are second-line; NSAIDs are ineffective.
keyClue: Bilateral burning neuropathic foot pain at night in long-standing poorly controlled diabetes
commonExamTrap: Choosing an NSAID for neuropathic pain — NSAIDs target inflammatory pain, not the central sensitisation mechanism of neuropathy.
missedConcept: Neuropathic pain requires centrally acting agents (TCAs, SNRIs, gabapentinoids); anti-inflammatory drugs are ineffective.

sourceLecture: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman who works as a data entry clerk presents with a 6-month history of tingling and numbness in her right thumb, index finger, middle finger, and lateral half of the ring finger. Symptoms are worse at night and she wakes to shake her hand for relief. Examination reveals reduced light touch over the affected fingers, positive Tinel's sign at the wrist, and a positive Phalen's test. There is no thenar wasting. Which investigation would best confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Nerve conduction study of the median nerve across the wrist
Plain X-ray of the right wrist
Ultrasound of the right shoulder for rotator cuff pathology
Blood test for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
explanation: The clinical presentation is classic for carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) — compression of the median nerve at the wrist causing sensory symptoms in the median nerve distribution (thumb, index, middle, and lateral ring finger), nocturnal worsening, and positive provocative tests (Tinel's, Phalen's). Nerve conduction study (NCS) demonstrates slowing of median nerve conduction across the wrist and is the gold-standard confirmatory investigation, assessing both sensory and motor latencies.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI cervical spine would be appropriate for cervical radiculopathy, which typically causes neck pain and a broader dermatomal distribution; this presentation is localised to the median nerve territory at the wrist.
Nerve conduction study is correct — it is the gold-standard investigation that confirms median nerve compression at the wrist with prolonged distal motor and sensory latencies.
Plain X-ray of the wrist may show fractures or calcification but does not confirm nerve compression.
Shoulder pathology would not explain finger numbness in a median nerve distribution with positive wrist provocation tests.
Rheumatoid factor is appropriate if systemic inflammatory arthritis is suspected as a secondary cause of CTS, but NCS is still the primary confirmatory test for CTS itself.
clinicalPearl: Nerve conduction studies are the gold standard for confirming CTS, demonstrating prolonged distal sensory and motor latencies of the median nerve across the carpal tunnel.
keyClue: Tingling in thumb/index/middle/lateral ring finger, nocturnal, positive Tinel's and Phalen's signs
commonExamTrap: Ordering cervical MRI first — C6 radiculopathy can mimic CTS but lacks wrist-specific signs; NCS localises the lesion to the wrist.
missedConcept: Carpal tunnel syndrome is median nerve compression at the wrist; NCS confirms diagnosis with prolonged median nerve conduction across the carpal tunnel.

clinicalStem: A 54-year-old obese man (BMI 36 kg/m²) with hypertension and type 2 diabetes presents with his wife, who reports that he snores loudly and has observed episodes where he stops breathing during sleep followed by a gasp. He complains of excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth score 16/24) and wakes unrefreshed. He also has morning headaches. Examination shows a crowded oropharynx (Mallampati class III) and neck circumference of 44 cm. Which investigation will both confirm the diagnosis and quantify severity?
explanation: Polysomnography (PSG) is the gold-standard investigation for obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA). It measures the apnoea-hypopnoea index (AHI), classifies severity (mild 5–15, moderate 15–30, severe >30 events/hour), and provides data on oxygen saturation, sleep stages, arousals, and respiratory effort. Home sleep apnoea testing (level III) is an acceptable alternative in straightforward cases, but full PSG remains the reference standard.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Overnight pulse oximetry can screen for desaturations but does not confirm OSA or quantify the AHI; it cannot distinguish obstructive from central apnoea.
Polysomnography is correct — it is the gold standard, measuring AHI and sleep architecture to confirm diagnosis and guide treatment decisions.
Ambulatory BP monitoring assesses hypertension control but does not diagnose OSA; hypertension is a consequence of untreated OSA.
Multiple sleep latency test (MSLT) measures sleep onset latency and is used to diagnose narcolepsy, not OSA.
The Epworth Sleepiness Scale is a validated screening questionnaire for daytime sleepiness but cannot confirm OSA or provide AHI data.
clinicalPearl: Polysomnography is the gold standard for OSA, providing the AHI that drives treatment decisions — CPAP is indicated for moderate-severe OSA (AHI ≥15) or mild OSA with symptoms.
keyClue: Witnessed apnoeas, loud snoring, Epworth score 16, obesity, and morning headaches
commonExamTrap: Choosing MSLT — this test diagnoses narcolepsy (rapid sleep onset, REM intrusion), not OSA.
missedConcept: Polysomnography measures AHI to classify OSA severity; morning headaches in OSA result from nocturnal hypercapnia.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old university student presents with a 2-year history of irresistible daytime sleep attacks occurring 3–4 times per day, each lasting 10–20 minutes, from which she wakes refreshed. She also reports episodes of sudden bilateral leg weakness causing her to collapse, triggered exclusively by laughing at jokes or hearing surprising news, with no loss of consciousness during these episodes. In addition, she describes vivid dream-like hallucinations at sleep onset and brief inability to move upon waking. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: This presentation fulfils the tetrad of narcolepsy type 1: (1) excessive daytime sleepiness with refreshing naps, (2) cataplexy — emotion-triggered loss of muscle tone without loss of consciousness, (3) hypnagogic hallucinations at sleep onset, and (4) sleep paralysis. Narcolepsy type 1 is caused by loss of orexin (hypocretin)-producing neurons in the hypothalamus. It is confirmed by MSLT showing a mean sleep latency <8 minutes and ≥2 sleep-onset REM periods (SOREMPs), and low CSF orexin levels.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Idiopathic hypersomnia causes excessive sleepiness but naps are not refreshing and there is no cataplexy, hypnagogic hallucinations, or sleep paralysis.
Narcolepsy type 1 is correct — the full tetrad (EDS, cataplexy, hypnagogic hallucinations, sleep paralysis) is present and emotion-triggered collapse without LOC is pathognomonic for cataplexy.
Atonic epilepsy (drop attacks) involves loss of consciousness, is not emotion-triggered, and is associated with EEG abnormalities; the patient remains fully conscious during episodes.
OSA causes EDS due to fragmented nocturnal sleep; naps are not refreshing and there is no cataplexy or accessory symptoms.
Kleine-Levin syndrome causes recurrent episodes of hypersomnia lasting days to weeks with cognitive and behavioural changes; it does not present with cataplexy or daily sleep attacks.
clinicalPearl: Emotion-triggered bilateral muscle weakness without loss of consciousness is pathognomonic of cataplexy — the cardinal symptom that distinguishes narcolepsy type 1 from type 2.
keyClue: Refreshing sleep attacks + emotion-triggered collapse without LOC + hypnagogic hallucinations + sleep paralysis
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing epilepsy — atonic seizures involve LOC and are not selectively triggered by emotion; narcolepsy patients remain fully conscious during cataplexy.
missedConcept: Narcolepsy type 1 tetrad: EDS with refreshing naps, cataplexy, hypnagogic/hypnopompic hallucinations, sleep paralysis — caused by orexin/hypocretin deficiency.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease on haemodialysis presents with a 6-month history of uncomfortable creeping and crawling sensations in both legs at rest, particularly in the evenings and nights, which are partially relieved by walking or moving her legs. The symptoms cause significant sleep disturbance. She also reports heavy menstrual bleeding due to uterine fibroids. Serum ferritin is 14 µg/L. Haemoglobin is 9.2 g/dL. Which is the most appropriate initial treatment for her restless legs syndrome?
options: Start pramipexole (dopamine agonist) 0.088 mg at night
Prescribe oral iron supplementation to correct iron deficiency
explanation: This patient has iron-deficiency anaemia (ferritin 14 µg/L) with end-stage renal disease (ESRD) as the likely cause of secondary RLS. Iron deficiency is the most important reversible secondary cause of RLS. In a patient with ESRD on haemodialysis, oral iron absorption is unreliable due to gastrointestinal side effects and reduced absorption; intravenous iron infusion is therefore the preferred route to adequately replenish stores. Treating iron deficiency before starting dopaminergic agents is the recommended approach.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pramipexole (dopamine agonist) is effective but is not the first step when a reversible cause (iron deficiency) has been identified; correcting iron first is mandatory.
Oral iron is appropriate in primary care RLS with iron deficiency but is poorly absorbed and poorly tolerated in ESRD; IV iron is the correct route in this context.
Gabapentin is a second-line option for RLS, particularly useful when pain is prominent, but treating the underlying iron deficiency takes priority.
Intravenous iron infusion is correct — iron deficiency is the identified reversible cause and IV iron is the appropriate route in ESRD where oral absorption is impaired.
Clonazepam may improve sleep quality in RLS but does not address the underlying pathophysiology and has significant side effects; it is not a recommended first-line treatment.
clinicalPearl: Always check and correct iron deficiency before starting dopaminergic agents in RLS — ferritin <50 µg/L should be corrected; IV iron is preferred in renal failure.
keyClue: RLS symptoms with ferritin 14 µg/L and ESRD on haemodialysis — iron deficiency is the reversible cause
commonExamTrap: Jumping to dopamine agonists without addressing iron deficiency — correcting iron deficiency first is the recommended approach.
missedConcept: Iron deficiency is the commonest reversible secondary cause of RLS; in ESRD, IV rather than oral iron is preferred due to poor GI absorption.

clinicalStem: A 17-year-old girl with a 2-year history of anorexia nervosa (restricting type) is admitted for medical stabilisation. Her BMI is 13.5 kg/m². She has been severely restricting intake for weeks. On day 3 of cautious nutritional rehabilitation, her blood results show: phosphate 0.4 mmol/L (normal 0.8–1.5), potassium 2.8 mmol/L, magnesium 0.6 mmol/L. On examination she develops new peripheral oedema and appears mildly confused. ECG shows prolonged QT interval. What syndrome does this represent and what is the priority management?
options: Refeeding syndrome — stop nutrition immediately and replace electrolytes
Refeeding syndrome — slow or pause feeding and aggressively replace phosphate, potassium, and magnesium
Wernicke's encephalopathy — give intramuscular thiamine immediately
Hypokalaemia from laxative abuse — oral potassium supplementation
explanation: Refeeding syndrome occurs when nutrition is reintroduced to a severely malnourished patient, causing a surge in insulin that drives phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells, resulting in severe hypophosphataemia and electrolyte depletion. This causes cardiac arrhythmias, oedema, neuromuscular dysfunction, and potentially death. Management involves slowing or temporarily pausing feeding, aggressive intravenous replacement of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium, and continuous cardiac monitoring. Nutrition should NOT be stopped entirely — it should be reduced in rate while electrolytes are corrected.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hypoalbuminaemia is present in malnutrition but this clinical picture with low phosphate after refeeding initiation is refeeding syndrome; albumin infusion does not address the primary problem.
Stopping nutrition entirely is incorrect — feeding should be reduced but not stopped, as complete cessation risks further catabolism; the priority is electrolyte replacement.
This is the correct answer — refeeding syndrome requires slowing feeding and aggressively replacing phosphate (IV), potassium, and magnesium with cardiac monitoring.

Wernicke's encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency) can occur in this context and thiamine should be given prophylactically BEFORE refeeding, but the electrolyte picture here is characteristic of refeeding syndrome rather than Wernicke's.
Hypokalaemia from laxative abuse is plausible but the combination of low phosphate, low potassium, and low magnesium appearing on day 3 of refeeding is the classic presentation of refeeding syndrome.
clinicalPearl: Refeeding syndrome: phosphate drops sharply within 72 hours of refeeding in severely malnourished patients — monitor and replace electrolytes; slow feeding but do not stop it entirely.
keyClue: Severe anorexia, day 3 of refeeding, hypophosphataemia + hypokalaemia + hypomagnesaemia + oedema + QT prolongation
commonExamTrap: Stopping feeding entirely — the correct response is to slow feeding and replace electrolytes, not to stop nutrition.
missedConcept: Refeeding syndrome is an insulin-mediated shift of electrolytes into cells upon refeeding; hypophosphataemia is the hallmark and can cause cardiac arrest.
redFlagReason: Refeeding syndrome with severe hypophosphataemia can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias; failure to monitor and replace electrolytes is life-threatening in severely malnourished patients.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman presents to her GP complaining of fatigue, palpitations, and dental sensitivity. She denies weight problems, stating her weight is normal (BMI 22 kg/m²). Closer questioning reveals she regularly consumes large quantities of food in secret within short periods, feeling out of control, followed by self-induced vomiting to prevent weight gain. She has done this at least twice weekly for the past 6 months. Examination reveals bilateral parotid gland enlargement and calluses on the dorsum of her right hand. Bloods show hypokalaemia (2.9 mmol/L) and a metabolic alkalosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Bulimia nervosa is characterised by recurrent binge eating episodes (large amounts with loss of control) followed by compensatory behaviours (self-induced vomiting, laxatives, excessive exercise) to prevent weight gain, occurring at least once weekly for 3 months, with body weight typically maintained in the normal range. Parotid swelling (sialadenosis from repeated vomiting stimulation), Russell's sign (calluses on knuckles from inducing vomiting), hypokalaemia, and metabolic alkalosis from loss of hydrochloric acid through vomiting are pathognomonic features.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Anorexia nervosa binge-purging type requires significantly low body weight (BMI typically <17.5 or equivalent); this patient has a normal BMI of 22, excluding this diagnosis.
Binge-eating disorder involves recurrent binges without regular compensatory behaviours; this patient clearly self-induces vomiting, indicating bulimia nervosa.
Bulimia nervosa is correct — normal weight, binge-purge cycles ≥twice weekly for 6 months, parotid hypertrophy, Russell's sign, hypokalaemia, and metabolic alkalosis are all characteristic.
ARFID involves restriction/avoidance of food based on sensory characteristics or fear of adverse consequences, without bingeing or purging behaviours.
Depression with hyperphagia does not involve compensatory purging behaviours or the physical signs of repeated self-induced vomiting.
clinicalPearl: Normal body weight with binge-purge cycles, parotid enlargement, Russell's sign, and hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis = bulimia nervosa.
keyClue: Normal BMI, binge-purge cycles, parotid enlargement, Russell's sign, hypokalaemia, metabolic alkalosis
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing anorexia nervosa binge-purging type — anorexia requires significantly low body weight; normal BMI distinguishes bulimia nervosa.
missedConcept: Bulimia nervosa is maintained at normal body weight; metabolic alkalosis and hypokalaemia result from hydrochloric acid loss through vomiting.

clinicalStem: A 16-year-old girl with a 3-year history of anorexia nervosa is brought to the emergency department by her parents. Her BMI is 12.0 kg/m², she is bradycardic (HR 38 bpm), hypotensive (BP 80/50 mmHg), and hypothermic (35.2°C). She is alert but repeatedly refuses any medical treatment or hospital admission. Her parents consent to treatment. She states she understands the consequences of her illness and chooses not to be treated. Which statement best reflects the correct ethical and legal approach in this situation?
options: As she is 16 and demonstrates understanding, her refusal must be respected and she should be discharged
Her parents' consent is sufficient; treatment can proceed on the basis of parental consent alone
Treatment can proceed under emergency powers or appropriate Mental Health/Mental Capacity legislation despite her refusal, given the immediate risk to life
Refer to adult psychiatry for a capacity assessment before any treatment is given
Provide fluids only as a compromise and discharge once stabilised
explanation: This patient has a life-threatening presentation of anorexia nervosa (BMI 12, bradycardia HR 38, hypotension, hypothermia) and is refusing treatment. At 16, she has the right to consent to treatment under Gillick competence/Fraser guidelines, but refusal by a competent minor in a life-threatening situation can be overridden by the courts, parents, or clinicians acting under emergency powers or the Mental Health Act (MHA). Anorexia nervosa frequently impairs insight and decision-making capacity due to the ego-syntonic nature of the illness. Emergency medical treatment can and should be given to preserve life.
wrongAnswerExplanations: While 16-year-olds may consent to treatment, the right to refuse life-saving treatment can be overridden in those under 18 when there is imminent risk to life — absolute refusal is not ethically or legally correct here.
Parental consent alone is insufficient to override a competent minor's refusal in all circumstances, though parental consent is relevant and treatment can proceed under MHA or emergency powers.

This is the correct answer — in an immediately life-threatening situation, treatment can proceed under emergency powers or MHA/MCA despite refusal; anorexia nervosa also impairs capacity in this context.
Waiting for a formal capacity assessment before any treatment in a haemodynamically compromised patient (HR 38, BP 80/50) is dangerous and would cause avoidable harm.
Providing fluids only and discharging an acutely medically unstable patient with a potentially fatal presentation is clinically and ethically inadequate.
clinicalPearl: In minors with life-threatening anorexia nervosa refusing treatment, emergency treatment can proceed under the Mental Health Act or emergency powers — the right to refuse does not include the right to die for under-18s.
keyClue: Life-threatening haemodynamic compromise (HR 38, BP 80/50, hypothermia, BMI 12) with treatment refusal in a 16-year-old
commonExamTrap: Assuming Gillick competence gives a 16-year-old absolute right to refuse life-saving treatment — this is not the case in English law for those under 18.
missedConcept: Anorexia nervosa impairs decision-making capacity due to its ego-syntonic nature; emergency treatment of a life-threatening presentation can be legally authorised even against stated wishes.
redFlagReason: Haemodynamically unstable anorexia nervosa (HR 38, BP 80/50, BMI 12) carries imminent risk of cardiac arrest; failing to treat is potentially fatal.
